title: PublishPort with not-yet-started podman-bridge IP fails with bind: cannot assign requested address
body:
### Issue Description

When `podman run`-ing a container that uses `--publish BridgeIP::`, which hasn't been established yet, this fails with 5.4.2, which worked fine on 4.3.1.

### Steps to reproduce the issue


```
[Unit]
Description=Valkey Container

[Container]
Image=docker.io/valkey/valkey:alpine
AutoUpdate=registry
PublishPort=10.88.0.1:6379:6379
Volume=/root/valkey:/data

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

Manually running the generated command:
```
root@test:~# ip a
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 ::1/128 scope host noprefixroute 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc fq_codel state UP group default qlen 1000
    link/ether bc:24:11:bd:71:1b brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    altname enp6s18
    altname enxbc2411bd711b
    inet 172.19.66.70/24 metric 100 brd 172.19.66.255 scope global dynamic eth0
       valid_lft 83195sec preferred_lft 83195sec
    inet6 fe80::be24:11ff:febd:711b/64 scope link proto kernel_ll 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
root@test:~# /usr/bin/podman run --rm --cgroups=split --sdnotify=conmon -v /root/valkey:/data --label io.containers.autoupdate=registry --publish 10.88.0.1:6379:6379 docker.io/valkey/valkey:alpine
Error: cannot listen on the TCP port: listen tcp4 10.88.0.1:6379: bind: cannot assign requested address
```

### Describe the results you received

```
Apr 30 23:59:13 vikunja systemd[1]: Starting valkey.service - Valkey Container...
Apr 30 23:59:13 vikunja podman[1524]: 2025-04-30 23:59:13.653139178 +0000 UTC m=+0.058217513 image pull 19acd301a07e65170d0aa3bf67ec3f6980cd515bdc260086c7af23ac7ab59bb4 docker.io/valkey/valkey:alpine
Apr 30 23:59:13 vikunja podman[1524]: 2025-04-30 23:59:13.881535141 +0000 UTC m=+0.286613448 container create ef25953fa58108858ab1ffa1f478d7cebce3f19ddc10b0e7ec7ddbf2d4132115 (image=docker.io/valkey/valkey:alpine, name=systemd-valkey, PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=valkey.service, io.containers.autoupdate=registry)
Apr 30 23:59:13 vikunja podman[1524]: 2025-04-30 23:59:13.99692848 +0000 UTC m=+0.402006861 container remove ef25953fa58108858ab1ffa1f478d7cebce3f19ddc10b0e7ec7ddbf2d4132115 (image=docker.io/valkey/valkey:alpine, name=systemd-valkey, PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=valkey.service, io.containers.autoupdate=registry)
Apr 30 23:59:13 vikunja valkey[1524]: Error: cannot listen on the TCP port: listen tcp4 10.88.0.1:6379: bind: cannot assign requested address
Apr 30 23:59:14 vikunja systemd[1]: valkey.service: Main process exited, code=exited, status=126/n/a
```

### Describe the results you expected

The generated `podman run`-command works just fine with Podman 4.3.1:
```
/usr/bin/podman run --rm --cgroups=split --sdnotify=conmon -v /root/valkey:/data --label io.containers.autoupdate=registry --publish 10.88.0.1:6379:6379 docker.io/valkey/valkey:alpine
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Failing: Debian Trixie
Working: Debian Bookworm

comments.body:
I can't reproduce on Fedora 42 and Podman 5.4.2:

```
/usr/bin/podman run --rm --cgroups=split --sdnotify=conmon --label io.containers.autoupdate=registry --publish <IP>:6379:6379 docker.io/valkey/valkey:alpine
1:M 01 May 2025 08:22:47.943 # WARNING Memory overcommit must be enabled! Without it, a background save or replication may fail under low memory condition. Being disabled, it can also cause failures without low memory condition, see https://github.com/jemalloc/jemalloc/issues/1328. To fix this issue add 'vm.overcommit_memory = 1' to /etc/sysctl.conf and then reboot or run the command 'sysctl vm.overcommit_memory=1' for this to take effect.
1:M 01 May 2025 08:22:47.943 * oO0OoO0OoO0Oo Valkey is starting oO0OoO0OoO0Oo
1:M 01 May 2025 08:22:47.943 * Valkey version=8.1.1, bits=64, commit=00000000, modified=0, pid=1, just started
1:M 01 May 2025 08:22:47.943 # Warning: no config file specified, using the default config. In order to specify a config file use valkey-server /path/to/valkey.conf
1:M 01 May 2025 08:22:47.944 * monotonic clock: POSIX clock_gettime
1:M 01 May 2025 08:22:47.944 * Running mode=standalone, port=6379.
1:M 01 May 2025 08:22:47.944 * Server initialized
1:M 01 May 2025 08:22:47.944 * Ready to accept connections tcp
```
where `<IP>` is an IPv4 I have on an interface, found with `ip a`.

Yeah, that’s no the issue.
It’s when used with the host IP of a podman network, and that network hasn’t been established yet.

I.e. the default podman network uses 10.88.0.1.

That is expected, we must bind ports before we do the actual network setup see https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/77081df8cdf0d2a68d52eebacc35d3e6a1df7660

I don't see a way to avoid that in podman, and when running in a user namespace or rootless it never worked before that either.

Why do you try to bind the bridge ip exactly?

> That is expected, we must bind ports before we do the actual network setup

So, this bug fixed - from my perspective regressed - binding ports since 5.3, due to possible concurrency conflicts.

> I don't see a way to avoid that in podman

I don't know what exactly RHEL-50746 is about (I don't have access), but when the current (5.3+) order of network operations (bind ports, start networks) should remain, then maybe `IP_FREEBIND` is an option:
```
       IP_FREEBIND (since Linux 2.4)
              If enabled, this boolean option allows binding to an IP
              address that is nonlocal or does not (yet) exist.  This
              permits listening on a socket, without requiring the
              underlying network interface or the specified dynamic IP
              address to be up at the time that the application is trying
              to bind to it.  This option is the per-socket equivalent of
              the ip_nonlocal_bind /proc interface.
```

> when running in a user namespace or rootless it never worked before that either

Wasn't concerned about that one, my primary use case are system containers, see below.

> Why do you try to bind the bridge ip exactly?

I have "Application VMs", that are VMs, hosting a couple of commonly shared resources (i.e. I don't want to have three MariaDB/PostgreSQL, Redis/Valkey containers running) on one host, so I have one container of ingress/reverse proxy, database server, valkey, and then a couple of containers sharing these - so Pods are out. I could create new networks with DNS enabled, or change the Podman default to enable DNS, but that requires that I remember doing this.
So, with the default configuration of Podman, I just published to the host bridge IP ports that should be available to other containers. These containers are then configured to connect to host.containers.internals:ServicePort, so that I don't have global (0.0.0.0/0) port bindings that I have to restrict access to.
Possible workaround is using `ip_nonlocal_bind=1`, but I'd have to redo this for every host VM.

If the above socket bind flag is an option for keeping the 4.x behavior, I'd be grateful, but I do acknowledge, that option might've been discussed non-publically already (in RH Issues), or reintroduces behaviors, that should've been fixed with 5.3.
I'd like to know, whether there are more people like me, who would be caught by surprise with the 5.3-changeset, when upgrading their systems in a couple of months, and some containers fail to start, because they also bind to bridge local ips - but only sometimes, because when these networks were brought up by another container, these port binds would just work.
From that perspective:
- I start container B, it fails with non-local port bind
- If I start container A, which is on the same Podman network as container B, and then start container B, it works

would be a surprising behavior.


Edit:
From my shallow look at the code, using IP_FREEBIND would require switching from `net.ListenUDP` and `net.ListenTCP` to [`net.ListenConfig.Listen`](https://pkg.go.dev/net#ListenConfig.Listen)/`net.ListenConfig.ListenPacket` respectively, which sets `syscall.SetsockoptInt(?, syscall.SOL_IP /* or syscall.SOL_IPV6 */, syscall.IP_FREEBIND, 1)` using net.ListenConfig.Control-callback.

> IP_FREEBIND

That is not an option, we must error when a non host ip is specified.

The underlying thing is that binding after we configure the network was always incorrect because we redirect via firewall which then means if someone else already bound the port we hijacked it and only failed later which is just a clear bug that had to be fixed. There isn't any important conversation in that bug.


Yes it is surprising that you cannot bind for the first container that starts on the given network, if you start a another one without ports it will always work so maybe do that for now. In general using named networks and dns for inter container communication is always recommend over forwarding ports on the host.
Also `host.containers.internal` has no real definition on what host ip address it should point to, just that it needs to point to some address on the host. So your assumption is already relying on the implementation detail that we are using the bridge ip for this host name.

So overall I think the most stable solution if you really want to use host ports to communicate between containers is to create your own private virtual interface with an address and then bind and connect to that address.

Now I do agree that is a regression for your specific use case and I tend to take regressions seriously but given it never worked as rootless and with a user namespace I see little reason to make this work again. Our network setup cannot easily be changed for this, to make this work is so that we first setup the bridge then bind ports then setup the rest of the container networking which would require a full on rewrite and it is not clear to me how this should look then.
Also it is the first time I have ever seen someone bind the specific bridge ip of the network so I don't think it is a common pattern and the fix has actually been released in podman v5.2.0 which means it is out for 9 months now and only reported now.
Fair, I'll leave it at that - pre-5.2 behavior was technically faulty, and Podman DNS-enabled network or a host local IP/Port, should be used instead.
